questions.question: What is the correct way to print 'Hello, World!' in Python?
questions.explanation: In Python, print() is the built-in function used to output text to the console. Unlike other languages, no import is needed — just call print() directly.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to define a function in Python?
questions.explanation: Python uses the 'def' keyword to define a function. Example: def is_leap(year): — then the function body is indented below it.

questions.question: What is the correct syntax for a for loop that goes from 0 to 4?
questions.explanation: range(0, 5) generates 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — the end value is exclusive. range(0, 4) would only go to 3, missing 4.

questions.question: How do you loop through a list called 'nums'?
questions.explanation: Python's 'for ... in ...' syntax directly iterates over every item in a list. 'foreach' and 'for...of' are JavaScript syntax and don't exist in Python.

questions.question: What does s.swapcase() do to the string 'Hello'?
questions.explanation: swapcase() swaps the case of every letter: uppercase becomes lowercase and lowercase becomes uppercase. 'H' → 'h', 'ello' → 'ELLO'.

questions.question: Which data structure uses square brackets [] in Python?
questions.explanation: Lists use square brackets [1, 2, 3]. Tuples use parentheses (1, 2, 3), sets use curly braces {1, 2, 3}, and dictionaries use {key: value}.

questions.question: What is the main difference between a list and a tuple?

options.label: b
options.text: Lists are immutable, tuples are mutable

options.label: c
options.text: Lists are mutable, tuples are immutable

questions.explanation: Lists [1,2,3] can be changed after creation (mutable). Tuples (1,2,3) cannot be modified after creation (immutable). Tuples are also faster and use less memory.

questions.question: What does list.append(x) do?

options.label: b
options.text: Adds x to the beginning of the list

options.label: c
options.text: Adds x to the end of the list

questions.explanation: append(x) always adds x to the end. To add to the beginning, use insert(0, x). To remove from the end, use pop().

questions.question: How do you print values on the same line inside a loop?
questions.explanation: The 'end' parameter of print() defaults to '\n' (newline). Setting end='' removes the newline so the next print continues on the same line.

questions.question: How do you format a float to exactly 2 decimal places using an f-string?
questions.explanation: f"{number:.2f}" formats the number as a float with exactly 2 decimal places. '%.2f' % number and '{:.2f}'.format(number) are the older equivalents.

questions.question: What does divmod(10, 3) return?
questions.explanation: divmod(a, b) returns a tuple (quotient, remainder). 10 // 3 = 3 and 10 % 3 = 1, so the result is (3, 1). Useful when you need both values at once.

questions.question: What is the difference between set.remove(x) and set.discard(x)?

options.label: a
options.text: remove() adds an element, discard() deletes it

options.label: b
options.text: remove() raises KeyError if x doesn't exist, discard() does not

options.label: c
options.text: discard() raises KeyError if x doesn't exist, remove() does not

questions.explanation: remove(x) raises a KeyError if x is not in the set. discard(x) silently does nothing if x is absent — use discard() when you're not sure if the element exists.

questions.question: What does s.intersection(t) return?

options.label: a
options.text: Elements in s but not in t

options.label: b
options.text: All elements from both s and t

options.label: c
options.text: Elements present in both s and t

options.label: d
options.text: Elements in either s or t but not both

questions.explanation: Intersection (A ∩ B) returns only elements that appear in both sets. s & t is shorthand. Union (A ∪ B) returns all elements from both, and symmetric_difference returns elements in one but not both.

questions.question: What does Counter from the collections module do?

options.label: a
options.text: Counts the number of keys in a dictionary

options.label: b
options.text: Counts occurrences of each element in a list and returns a dict-like object

options.label: c
options.text: Sorts a list in descending order

options.label: d
options.text: Removes duplicate elements from a list

questions.explanation: Counter([1, 2, 2, 3]) returns Counter({2: 2, 1: 1, 3: 1}) — each element mapped to how many times it appears. Very useful for frequency counting without writing loops.

questions.question: What is a deque in Python?

options.label: c
options.text: A double-ended queue that supports adding/removing from both ends

questions.explanation: collections.deque supports append/pop from the right and appendleft/popleft from the left, both in O(1) time. A regular list's insert(0, x) is O(n), making deque much faster for front operations.

questions.question: What does s.symmetric_difference(t) return?

options.label: a
options.text: Elements in both s and t

options.label: b
options.text: Elements in s but not in t

options.label: c
options.text: Elements in either s or t, but not both

options.label: d
options.text: The union of s and t

questions.explanation: Symmetric difference returns elements that are in one set OR the other, but NOT in both — it's the opposite of intersection. Think of it as 'exclusive or' for sets.

questions.question: What does str.isalnum() return for the string 'ab123'?

options.label: b
options.text: True, because all characters are letters or digits

options.label: c
options.text: False, because it contains both letters and numbers

options.label: d
options.text: True, only if the string is all letters

questions.explanation: isalnum() returns True if every character is alphanumeric (a-z, A-Z, or 0-9). 'ab123' passes. 'ab123#' would return False because '#' is not alphanumeric.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct list comprehension with a condition?

options.label: a
options.text: [i for i in range(10) where i % 2 == 0]

options.label: b
options.text: [i for i in range(10) if i % 2 == 0]

options.label: c
options.text: [i if i % 2 == 0 for i in range(10)]

options.label: d
options.text: (i for i in range(10) if i % 2 == 0)

questions.explanation: List comprehension syntax is: [expression for item in iterable if condition]. Option a) uses 'where' (SQL syntax, invalid). Option d) is a generator expression using () — not a list.

questions.question: How do you get all ordered permutations of length 2 from ['1','2','3']?
questions.explanation: permutations(iterable, r) returns all ordered r-length arrangements. Without r (option d), it returns full-length permutations (triples, not pairs). combinations() gives unordered pairs.

questions.question: What does the * (star) notation do in: *name, price = input().split()?

options.label: a
options.text: Multiplies all values in the list

options.label: b
options.text: Captures all but the last split value into a list called name

options.label: c
options.text: Unpacks only the first value into name

questions.explanation: The * (splat/star) operator in unpacking captures multiple values into a list. Here, all words except the last go into 'name' as a list, and the last word goes into 'price'. Useful when input has variable-length names.

questions.question: What does `typeof null` return in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: `typeof null === "object"` is a long-standing JavaScript bug that was never fixed for backwards compatibility. `null` is its own primitive type, but `typeof` incorrectly reports it as "object".

questions.question: Which keyword declares a variable that CANNOT be reassigned?
questions.explanation: `const` declares a variable that cannot be reassigned after initialization and must be given a value at declaration. `let` allows reassignment; `var` also allows reassignment.

questions.question: What value does a declared but uninitialized `let` variable hold?
questions.explanation: In JavaScript, `let age;` without an assignment automatically holds `undefined`. This means "this variable exists but has no value yet." It's different from `null`, which is an intentional empty value.

questions.question: What does `typeof "hello"` return?
questions.explanation: `typeof` returns a string describing the type. For text values it returns `"string"`. Other possible returns include `"number"`, `"boolean"`, `"undefined"`, `"object"`, and `"function"`.
`age` is declared but never assigned, so it holds `undefined`. `typeof undefined` returns the string `"undefined"` — note it is a string result, not the bare value `undefined`.
This is one of JavaScript's oldest quirks. `typeof null` returns `"object"` even though `null` is not an object — it's a historical bug that remains for backwards compatibility.
`x` holds the number `10` → `typeof x` is `"number"`. `y` holds the string `"10"` → `typeof y` is `"string"`. The quotes matter — `10` and `"10"` are completely different types in JavaScript.
`const` variables cannot be reassigned. Attempting to do so throws a `TypeError: Assignment to constant variable`. This is why `const` is used for values that should never change after creation.
Primitive values like numbers are copied by value, not by reference. `b = a` makes `b` a separate copy of `5`. Changing `b` to `10` has zero effect on `a`, which stays `5`.

questions.question: Which of these correctly declares a boolean in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: JavaScript booleans are the literal values `true` and `false` (lowercase). `"true"` is a string, `True` causes a ReferenceError (JavaScript is case-sensitive), and `1` is a number — truthy, but not the boolean `true`.

questions.question: What does `&&` (AND) do when the first condition is false?

options.label: c
options.text: Returns the first falsy value immediately

questions.explanation: `&&` short-circuits: if the first value is falsy it immediately returns that value without evaluating the right side. This is why `false && doSomething()` never calls `doSomething()`.

questions.question: What does `condition ? a : b` return when condition is false?
questions.explanation: The ternary operator is a compact if-else. When the condition is truthy it returns the value before `:`. When falsy it returns the value after `:`. So `false ? "yes" : "no"` returns `"no"`.

questions.question: Which operator checks if a value is greater than OR equal to another?
questions.explanation: `>=` means "greater than or equal to". It returns `true` if the left value is larger than or the same as the right. Note: `=>` is arrow function syntax, not a comparison operator.

questions.question: What is `||` (OR) used for?

options.label: b
options.text: Returns true if at least one condition is true

questions.explanation: `||` returns `true` if ANY operand is truthy and short-circuits on the first truthy value. Commonly used for fallbacks: `let name = input || "Guest"` — if `input` is falsy, `"Guest"` is used.
`75 >= 90` is false. `75 >= 70` is true, so `"B"` prints and the chain stops. Once one `else if` matches, the remaining branches are skipped entirely.
`5 > 3` is `true` AND `5 < 10` is `true`. Since both are truthy, `&&` returns `true`. The result is the boolean `true`, not the string `"true"`.
The ternary checks `20 >= 18` which is `true`, so the expression evaluates to `"Adult"`. That value is stored in `msg` and logged.
In JavaScript, `0` is a falsy value. When used as an `if` condition it behaves like `false`. The complete falsy values are: `0`, `""`, `null`, `undefined`, `NaN`, and `false`.

questions.question:
What is the output?
console.log(false || "hello");
questions.explanation: `||` returns the first truthy value it finds, not just `true`/`false`. `false` is falsy so it moves on. `"hello"` is truthy so it returns `"hello"`. This pattern powers default values like `let name = input || "Guest"`.

`==` (loose equality) converts types before comparing — `"5"` becomes `5`, so `5 == "5"` is `true`. `===` (strict equality) checks value AND type — number vs string, so it is `false`. Always prefer `===` to avoid surprises.

questions.question: What are the three parts of a `for` loop?
questions.explanation: A `for` loop: `for (initialization; condition; increment)`. Initialization runs once at the start, the condition is checked before each iteration, and the increment runs after each iteration body.

questions.question: How do you correctly loop through every element in `let items = [10, 20, 30]`?
questions.explanation: Arrays are zero-indexed: first element at index `0`, last at `length - 1`. Start at `i = 0` and use `i < items.length` (strictly less than) to cover every element without going out of bounds.

questions.question: What does `for...of` iterate over?
questions.explanation: `for (let item of array)` gives you each value directly on every iteration without needing an index. Works on arrays, strings, Sets, and other iterables. Use `for...in` for object keys.

questions.question: What does `for...in` iterate over?

options.label: b
options.text: The keys/property names of an object

questions.explanation: `for (let key in object)` iterates over the enumerable property names of an object. If used on an array it gives index numbers as strings — which is not recommended for arrays.
Starts at `i = 0`, runs while `i < 3`. Prints `0`, `1`, `2`, then `i` becomes `3`. The condition `3 < 3` is false so the loop exits. The value `3` is never printed.
`for...of` gives each value directly. `fruit` holds `"apple"` on the first pass, `"banana"` on the second, `"cherry"` on the third. Each is logged on its own line.
`for...in` iterates over property keys. `person` has two keys: `"name"` and `"age"`. Those key strings are logged. To get values you'd write `person[key]`.
The loop adds each `i` to `sum`: 0+1=1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10. The final logged value is `10`.
`continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next one. When `i === 1`, the `console.log` is skipped. So only `0` and `2` are printed.
Each element is multiplied by `2` before logging. `nums[0]=1→2`, `nums[1]=2→4`, `nums[2]=3→6`. Each result is logged separately, not as an array.

questions.question: When does a `while` loop stop running?
questions.explanation: A `while` loop checks its condition before each iteration. If the condition is `false` at any check the loop ends. If the condition starts as `false`, the loop body never runs at all.

questions.question: What is the key difference between `while` and `do...while`?

options.label: b
options.text: `do...while` always runs the body at least once

questions.explanation: In `while`, the condition is checked BEFORE the body — it may run zero times. In `do...while`, the body runs FIRST then the condition is checked — so it always runs at least once.

questions.question: Why must a `while` loop include an update statement?

options.label: c
options.text: Because JavaScript requires it by syntax

questions.explanation: If the condition never becomes `false`, the loop runs forever (infinite loop), crashing the browser. An update like `i++` changes the variable the condition depends on, eventually making it `false`.

questions.question: What does `break` do inside a loop?

options.label: d
options.text: Restarts the loop from the beginning

questions.explanation: `break` immediately terminates the entire loop regardless of whether the condition is still true. Execution jumps to the first line after the loop. Often used with `if` to exit when a target value is found.
Starts at `i = 0`. Each iteration prints `i` then increments. After printing `2`, `i` becomes `3`. `3 < 3` is false — loop exits. Output: `0`, `1`, `2`.

In `do...while`, the body runs BEFORE checking the condition. So `5` is printed and `i` becomes `6`. Then `6 < 3` is false — loop ends. Only `5` is printed even though the condition was false from the start.
The loop runs normally for `i = 0, 1, 2`. When `i === 3`, `break` immediately exits the loop before logging `3`. So only `0`, `1`, `2` are printed.
`i++` runs first on each pass. When `i === 3`, `continue` skips the log and goes back to the condition check. The loop still continues normally after that. Output: `1, 2` then skip `3`, then `4, 5`.

options.label: b
options.text: Error: condition must be a variable

questions.explanation: `while (true)` loops forever but `break` exits when `i === 3`. After `i` is incremented to `3`, break fires and code jumps to `console.log(i)` which prints `3`. This is a valid pattern for complex exit conditions.
Starting at `x=1`: logs `1`, doubles to `2`; logs `2`, doubles to `4`; logs `4`, doubles to `8`; logs `8`, doubles to `16`. Now `16 < 10` is false — loop ends. Output: `1, 2, 4, 8`.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a function parameter?

options.label: b
options.text: To pass data into the function when calling it

questions.explanation: Parameters are named inputs defined in the function declaration. When the function is called, arguments get assigned to those parameters, allowing the same function to work with different data each time.

questions.question: What does the `return` statement do?

options.label: a
options.text: Prints a value to the console

options.label: c
options.text: Ends the function and sends back a value

questions.explanation: `return` does two things: it immediately stops the function from running and sends a value back to wherever the function was called. Without a `return`, a function returns `undefined` by default.

questions.question: What does a function return if it has no `return` statement?
questions.explanation: Every JavaScript function returns a value. If no `return` statement is present (or `return` is used alone with no value), the function automatically returns `undefined`.

questions.question: What is a default parameter?

options.label: a
options.text: A function that always returns the same value

options.label: b
options.text: A parameter with a fallback value used when none is provided

options.label: c
options.text: A function that cannot be called without arguments

options.label: d
options.text: A parameter that ignores the passed value

questions.explanation: Default parameters let you specify a fallback value used when the caller doesn't pass an argument or passes `undefined`. Example: `function greet(name = "Guest")` — calling `greet()` uses `"Guest"` as the name.
`add(3, 4)` assigns `a = 3` and `b = 4`. The function returns `3 + 4 = 7`. `console.log(7)` prints `7`.
`greet()` passes no argument, so `name` uses default `"World"` → `"Hello, World!"`. `greet("Alex")` overrides the default → `"Hello, Alex!"`.
`double(5)` returns `5 * 2 = 10`. That value is stored in `result`. `console.log(result)` prints `10`.
`sayHi()` logs `"Hi!"` but has no `return` statement, so it returns `undefined`. That `undefined` is stored in `x`. The second `console.log` prints `undefined`.
JavaScript does NOT throw an error when you pass more arguments than parameters. Extra arguments are simply ignored. `a = 2`, `b = 3`, and `10` is discarded. The function returns `5`.
`check(5)` hits `if (x > 0)` which is true, so `return "positive"` executes — the function ends immediately. The `console.log("done")` is unreachable code and never runs.

questions.question: What keyword does an arrow function replace?
questions.explanation: Arrow functions are shorthand for regular functions. Instead of `function(x) { return x * 2; }` you write `(x) => x * 2`. The `function` keyword is replaced by the `=>` arrow after the parameters.

questions.question: What is "implicit return" in an arrow function?

options.label: b
options.text: The return value is undefined by default

options.label: c
options.text: A single-expression arrow function returns without writing `return`

options.label: d
options.text: The function returns the parameter name

questions.explanation: When an arrow function body is a single expression with no curly braces `{}`, the result is automatically returned. `(x) => x * 2` returns `x * 2` without writing `return`. Adding `{}` requires an explicit `return`.

questions.question: What happens if you use `{}` in an arrow function without a `return`?

options.label: d
options.text: It returns the last line automatically

questions.explanation: Curly braces `{}` create a function body block. Inside a block you must explicitly write `return`. Without it the function returns `undefined`. This is a common bug when adding curly braces to a previously implicit arrow function.

questions.question: Which is a valid arrow function that squares a number?
questions.explanation: Correct syntax: `(x) => x * x`. Option A mixes `function` keyword with arrow syntax. Option B is Java/Kotlin syntax. Option D uses a block but has no `return`, so it returns `undefined` instead of `x * x`.
`double` implicitly returns `x * 2`. Calling `double(6)` computes `6 * 2 = 12`. `console.log` prints `12`.
The curly braces create a function body, but there is no `return` statement. The template literal is evaluated but its result is discarded. The function returns `undefined`.
`add(3)`: `a=3`, `b` uses default `5` → returns `8`. `add(3, 7)`: `a=3`, `b=7` overrides default → returns `10`. Output: `8` then `10`.
`.filter()` keeps elements for which the callback returns `true`. `n % 2 === 0` is true for even numbers only. `2` and `4` pass the test, giving `[2, 4]`.
When an arrow function has exactly ONE parameter, the parentheses are optional. `x => x * x` is valid and equivalent to `(x) => x * x`. It returns `4 * 4 = 16`.

To implicitly return an object literal from an arrow function, wrap it in parentheses `()`. Without them, `{}` is interpreted as a function body block. The `()` tells JavaScript this is an expression to return.

questions.question: How do you access the FIRST element of `let items = [10, 20, 30]`?
questions.explanation: Arrays use zero-based indexing. The first element is at index `0`, the second at `1`, and so on. `items[0]` accesses `10`.

questions.question: What does the spread operator `...` do when used with two arrays?

options.label: c
options.text: Unpacks and combines their elements into a new array

questions.explanation: `...arr` unpacks all elements of `arr`. `let combined = [...a, ...b]` creates a new array with all elements of `a` followed by all elements of `b`. It's equivalent to `a.concat(b)` but more flexible.

questions.question: What does `array.concat(other)` return?

options.label: a
options.text: Modifies the original array in place

options.label: b
options.text: A new array combining both arrays

options.label: d
options.text: The last element of the array

questions.explanation: `.concat()` merges arrays WITHOUT modifying the originals. It returns a NEW array containing all elements of both. `[1,2].concat([3,4])` returns `[1,2,3,4]`.

questions.question: What does `array.length` return?

options.label: a
options.text: The index of the last element

options.label: d
options.text: The highest value in the array

questions.explanation: `.length` returns the count of elements. For `[10, 20, 30]`, `.length` is `3`. The last element's index is always `length - 1` (index `2` here), not `length` itself.
Index `0` = `10`, index `1` = `20`, index `2` = `30`. `nums[1]` accesses the element at index `1` which is `20`.
The spread operator unpacks both arrays. `...a` becomes `1, 2` and `...b` becomes `3, 4`. Combined into a new array: `[1, 2, 3, 4]`. This does NOT create a nested array.
`fruits.length` is `3`. `fruits[3 - 1]` = `fruits[2]` = `"cherry"`. Using `length - 1` is the reliable pattern to always get the last element of any array.
Array elements can be reassigned by index at any time. `nums[1] = 99` replaces the element at index `1` (which was `10`) with `99`. The array becomes `[5, 99, 15]`.

Unlike some languages, JavaScript does NOT throw an error for out-of-bounds access. Accessing a non-existent index returns `undefined`. This can cause silent bugs if you expect an error to catch mistakes.

Arrays are objects, stored by reference. `b = a` does NOT copy the array — both `a` and `b` point to the SAME array in memory. Changing `b[0]` changes `a[0]` too. To make a copy, use `let b = [...a]`.

questions.question: How do you access the `name` property of `let person = { name: "Alex", age: 16 }`?
questions.explanation: Dot notation `object.property` is the standard way to access object properties. Bracket notation with a string also works: `person["name"]`. But `person[name]` without quotes looks for a variable called `name`.

questions.question: What is a key-value pair in a JavaScript object?

options.label: b
options.text: A property name paired with its associated value

options.label: d
options.text: An array index and its element

questions.explanation: Objects store data as key-value pairs. The key is a string identifier (property name), and the value can be any type — number, string, boolean, array, or another object. E.g. `{ age: 16 }` where `"age"` is the key and `16` is the value.

questions.question: How do you add a new property to an existing object?

options.label: d
options.text: You cannot add properties after creation

questions.explanation: You can add new properties to objects at any time using dot notation or bracket notation with an assignment. `person.email = "alex@mail.com"` adds an `email` property.

questions.question: What does `for...in` return when used on an object?

options.label: c
options.text: The object converted to an array

questions.explanation: `for (let key in obj)` iterates over the enumerable property names. To get the value for each key, use `obj[key]` inside the loop.
`car.brand` accesses the `brand` property whose value is the string `"Toyota"`. That value is what gets logged.

`for...in` gives keys `"name"` and `"age"`. `person[key]` looks up each value. Concatenation produces `"name: Alex"` then `"age: 16"`. Note: `person.key` would not work — it looks for a literal property named `"key"`.
You can add new properties to objects after creation. `user.score = 100` adds the `score` property with value `100`. Accessing it immediately with `user.score` returns `100`.
Bracket notation `obj["b"]` is fully equivalent to dot notation `obj.b`. Both access the property named `"b"`, whose value is `2`.
Accessing a property that doesn't exist on an object returns `undefined` — no error is thrown. This is different from some other languages and can cause silent bugs if you misspell a property name.

Bracket notation `config[key]` evaluates the variable `key` first, getting `"theme"`. It then sets `config["theme"] = "dark"`. Dot notation `config.theme` and bracket `config["theme"]` both access the same property. This dynamic key pattern is very common.

questions.question: What does `.length` return on a string?

options.label: d
options.text: The index of the last character

questions.explanation: `.length` returns the total number of characters. `"hello".length` returns `5`. Spaces and punctuation count: `"hi there".length` is `8`.

questions.question: What does `.toUpperCase()` do?

options.label: a
options.text: Checks if the string is already uppercase

options.label: c
options.text: Returns a new string with all letters uppercased

options.label: d
options.text: Modifies the original string in place

questions.explanation: `.toUpperCase()` returns a NEW string with every letter converted to uppercase. Strings in JavaScript are immutable — the original is unchanged. `"hello".toUpperCase()` returns `"HELLO"`.

questions.question: What does `.includes()` return?

options.label: a
options.text: The index where the substring starts

options.label: b
options.text: A boolean — true if the substring is found

questions.explanation: `.includes(substring)` returns `true` if the substring is found anywhere in the string, `false` otherwise. `"hello world".includes("world")` returns `true`.

questions.question: What does `.trim()` remove?

options.label: a
options.text: All spaces everywhere in the string

questions.explanation: `.trim()` removes whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) from the START and END of a string only — NOT from the middle. `"  hello  ".trim()` returns `"hello"`.
`str[0]` accesses the first character at index `0`, which is `"J"`. `"JavaScript"` has 10 characters (J-a-v-a-S-c-r-i-p-t), so `.length` is `10`.
`.trim()` removes the leading and trailing spaces. The space between `Hello` and `World` remains — only outer whitespace is stripped. Result: `"Hello World"`.
`"banana".includes("nan")` — `"nan"` appears in `ba-nan-a` → `true`. `.toUpperCase()` converts every letter → `"BANANA"`.
`.split(" ")` splits by spaces, creating `["hello", "world"]`. `parts[1]` accesses index `1` which is `"world"`.

Strings in JavaScript are immutable. You cannot change individual characters by index assignment. `str[0] = "J"` silently does nothing. `str` remains `"Hello"`. Use `.replace()` or string rebuilding to modify strings.

`+` with a string performs concatenation — `"5" + 3` → `"53"`. But `-` has no string version, so JavaScript coerces `"5"` to the number `5` and computes `5 - 3 = 2`. This is type coercion and a common source of bugs.

questions.question: What does the `%` (modulo) operator return?

options.label: a
options.text: The result of division as a float

questions.explanation: `%` gives the remainder left over after integer division. `10 % 3 = 1` because 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1. Commonly used to check if a number is even: `n % 2 === 0`.

questions.question: What does `Math.max()` do?

options.label: c
options.text: Returns the largest number among its arguments

questions.explanation: `Math.max(3, 8, 1, 5)` returns `8`. When used with an array, spread it first: `Math.max(...arr)`.

questions.question: What does `Math.round()` do?
questions.explanation: `Math.round(4.7)` returns `5`, `Math.round(4.4)` returns `4`. Rule: decimal >= 0.5 rounds up; below 0.5 rounds down. `Math.floor` always rounds down, `Math.ceil` always rounds up.

questions.question: What does the `/` operator return in JavaScript for non-even divisions?

options.label: d
options.text: JavaScript rounds to the nearest integer

questions.explanation: JavaScript's `/` always returns a float when the result isn't a whole number. `10 / 3` returns `3.333...`, not `3`. Use `Math.floor(10 / 3)` to get integer division.
`10 % 3`: 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder **1**. `10 % 2`: 10 ÷ 2 = 5 remainder **0**. Remainder `0` means the number divides evenly — the standard even-number check.
`...candles` spreads the array elements as individual arguments: `Math.max(3, 8, 1, 6, 5)`. The maximum value is `8`.
`Math.round(4.5)` → `5` (halfway always rounds up). `Math.round(4.4)` → `4` (below halfway rounds down).
`7 / 2 = 3.5` — JavaScript returns a float. `Math.floor(3.5) = 3` — floor always rounds DOWN. Combine `/` with `Math.floor()` to get integer division.

questions.question:
What is the output?
console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3);
questions.explanation: Classic floating-point precision issue. `0.1 + 0.2` in binary floating point equals `0.30000000000000004`, not exactly `0.3`. So the strict equality check fails. Use `Math.abs(a - b) < 0.0001` for float comparisons.
`5 % 5 = 0` — any number modulo itself is `0` (divides evenly). `3 % 7 = 3` — when the dividend is smaller than the divisor, the remainder IS the dividend (7 goes into 3 zero times, leaving 3).

questions.question: What makes a `Set` different from an array?

options.label: a
options.text: A Set is ordered by value automatically

options.label: c
options.text: A Set automatically removes duplicate values

options.label: d
options.text: A Set has a fixed size

questions.explanation: A `Set` only stores unique values. If you try to add a value that already exists, it's silently ignored. `new Set([1, 2, 2, 3])` contains just `{1, 2, 3}`. Arrays allow duplicates; Sets do not.

questions.question: How do you add a value to a Set?
questions.explanation: Sets use `.add()`, not `.push()` (which is for arrays). `mySet.add(5)` adds `5` to the set. If `5` is already present, nothing changes — Sets are deduplicated automatically.

questions.question: How do you check if a value exists in a Set?
questions.explanation: Sets use `.has()` for membership checks — returns `true` if found, `false` otherwise. Arrays use `.includes()`. This is one of the key API differences between Sets and arrays.

questions.question: How do you get the number of elements in a Set?
questions.explanation: Sets use `.size` (not `.length`). `new Set([1, 2, 3]).size` returns `3`. This is a subtle but important difference from arrays.
`new Set()` removes all duplicates. `[1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]` has three unique values: `1`, `2`, `3`. The Set contains 3 elements, so `.size` returns `3`.
`20` already exists in the Set. `.add(20)` has no effect — Sets silently ignore duplicate additions. The size stays `3`.
`"b"` is in the Set → `.has("b")` returns `true`. `"z"` is not in the Set → `.has("z")` returns `false`.
`new Set(nums)` creates `{1, 2, 3}` (deduped). Spreading it with `[...set]` converts it back to a plain array `[1, 2, 3]`. This is the standard one-liner for removing duplicates from an array.
Sets use strict equality (`===`) to check for duplicates. The number `1` and the string `"1"` are different types — they are NOT `===` equal. So both are stored and `.size` is `2`.
Sets preserve insertion order. Elements are iterated in the order they were added, not sorted. `3` was inserted first, so it comes first. Sets ARE iterable with `for...of`.

questions.question: What is a nested loop?

options.label: a
options.text: A loop that skips every other iteration

options.label: c
options.text: A loop placed inside another loop

questions.explanation: A nested loop is a loop inside another loop. The inner loop runs completely for each single iteration of the outer loop. If outer runs 3 times and inner runs 3 times, the inner body executes 3 × 3 = 9 times.
The outer loop runs 3 times. For each of those, the inner loop runs 4 times. Total: 3 × 4 = 12 executions of the inner body. Nested loops multiply, they don't add.

questions.question: What is the typical time complexity of a double nested loop over n elements?
questions.explanation: If both loops iterate `n` times, the body runs `n × n = n²` times. For n=1000 that's 1,000,000 iterations. This is why deeply nested loops can slow programs significantly on large datasets.

questions.question: What is a common use case for nested loops?

options.label: b
options.text: Iterating over a 2D array (grid or matrix)

questions.explanation: Nested loops are ideal for 2D structures. The outer loop handles rows, the inner handles columns — every `grid[row][col]` combination can be reached. Also used for comparing pairs of elements in an array.
For `i=1`: inner runs `j=1` → `"1,1"`, then `j=2` → `"1,2"`. For `i=2`: `j=1` → `"2,1"`, then `j=2` → `"2,2"`. Total 4 lines in that order.
Each loop runs 3 times. `count++` runs 3 × 3 = 9 times. Starting at `0` and incrementing 9 times gives `9`.
`break` exits only the INNER loop. When `j === 1`, the inner loop exits but the outer continues. For each `i` (0, 1, 2), only `j = 0` prints before break fires. Output: `0 0`, `1 0`, `2 0`.
Outer loop iterates rows: `i=0` → row `[1,2]`, `i=1` → row `[3,4]`. Inner loop iterates columns. Output order: `grid[0][0]=1`, `grid[0][1]=2`, `grid[1][0]=3`, `grid[1][1]=4`.
The inner loop starts at `j = i`, not `j = 0`. For `i=0`: j prints 0,1,2. For `i=1`: j prints 1,2. For `i=2`: j prints 2 only. A triangular pattern of decreasing iterations.

questions.question: In a nested loop, what does `break` affect?

options.label: a
options.text: Both the inner and outer loop

options.label: c
options.text: Only the innermost loop it is placed in

questions.explanation: `break` exits only the loop it directly belongs to. If placed in the inner loop, it exits the inner loop and returns to the outer loop. To break both loops, use a boolean flag variable checked in the outer loop's condition.

questions.question: What is a recursive function?

options.label: a
options.text: A function that takes no arguments

options.label: b
options.text: A function that calls itself inside its own body

options.label: c
options.text: A function defined inside another function

options.label: d
options.text: A function that runs in reverse order

questions.explanation: A recursive function calls itself within its own body. Each call works on a smaller version of the problem until it reaches the base case. Recursion is an alternative to iteration for problems with repetitive substructure.

questions.question: What is the "base case" in recursion?

options.label: a
options.text: The first call to the function

options.label: b
options.text: The condition that stops the recursion

options.label: c
options.text: The return value of the last call

options.label: d
options.text: The largest input the function can handle

questions.explanation: The base case is the condition that, when true, returns immediately without making another recursive call. Without a base case, the function calls itself forever causing a stack overflow.

questions.question: What happens if a recursive function has no base case?

options.label: c
options.text: It causes a stack overflow error

options.label: d
options.text: JavaScript automatically stops it after 100 calls

questions.explanation: Each function call is added to the call stack. Without a base case, calls stack up indefinitely until JavaScript throws "Maximum call stack size exceeded" — a stack overflow.

questions.question: What is the "recursive case"?

options.label: a
options.text: The part where the function returns a fixed value

options.label: b
options.text: The part where the function calls itself with a modified argument

options.label: c
options.text: The part where the function accepts parameters

options.label: d
options.text: The condition that ends the recursion

questions.explanation: The recursive case calls the function again with a simpler or reduced input, moving the problem closer to the base case each time. Without progress toward the base case you'd still get infinite recursion.

`countdown(3)` logs `3` then calls `countdown(2)`, which logs `2` then calls `countdown(1)`, which logs `1` then calls `countdown(0)`. At `n === 0` the function returns without logging. Output: `3, 2, 1`.
Unwinds as: 4 × factorial(3) = 4 × 3 × factorial(2) = 4 × 3 × 2 × factorial(1) = 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 × factorial(0) = 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 × 1 = `24`.
This is the Fibonacci sequence. `mystery(5)` expands to the 5th Fibonacci number. The sequence is: 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, **5**. So the answer is `5`.

Each call removes the first character and appends it after the recursive result. `rev("abc")` = rev("bc") + "a" = (rev("c") + "b") + "a" = ((rev("") + "c") + "b") + "a" = "" + "c" + "b" + "a" = `"cba"`.

questions.question:
What is the exact output of this line?
print("Hello, World")
questions.explanation: print() outputs exactly what is inside the quotes, including punctuation. The comma is part of the string so it prints: Hello, World — no surrounding quotes in the output.

questions.question: What does `n % 2 == 1` check?
questions.explanation: % is the modulo operator — it gives the remainder after division. If n % 2 == 1, the remainder when dividing by 2 is 1, which means n is odd. Even numbers have remainder 0.
n=3. 3 % 2 == 1 is True (3 is odd), so the very first branch runs and prints "Weird". The entire else block is skipped.
n=4. 4 % 2 == 1 is False (4 is even). Goes to else. 4 >= 2 and 4 <= 5 is True → prints "Not Weird".
n=10. 10 % 2 == 1 is False. Goes to else. 10 >= 2 and 10 <= 5 is False. 10 >= 6 and 10 <= 20 is True → prints "Weird".
n=22. 22 % 2 == 1 is False (even). Goes to else. First two conditions are False. 22 > 20 is True → prints "Not Weird".

questions.question: What does the `and` keyword require?

options.label: a
options.text: At least one condition must be True

options.label: d
options.text: Exactly one condition must be True

questions.explanation: and returns True only if ALL conditions on both sides are True. If either is False, the whole expression is False. Example: n >= 2 and n <= 5 is only True if n satisfies BOTH conditions.

questions.question: What is the difference between `elif` and `else`?

options.label: b
options.text: elif checks a specific condition; else runs when all above conditions are False

options.label: c
options.text: else checks a condition; elif doesn't

questions.explanation: elif (else if) lets you check another specific condition with a test. else is a catch-all — it runs only when all preceding if and elif conditions were False. else never has a condition.

questions.question:
What is the output?
print("Hello", "World")
questions.explanation: When print() receives multiple arguments, it joins them with a single space by default. print("Hello", "World") outputs: Hello World — no comma, just a space between them.
x=15. 15 > 10 is True → prints "Big" and the rest of the chain is skipped immediately. Even though 15 > 5 is also True, elif only runs if the if was False. Only one branch ever executes.

questions.question: What values does `range(0, 5)` produce?
questions.explanation: range(start, stop) generates numbers from start up to but NOT including stop. range(0, 5) gives 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — the value 5 is never included.
range(0,4) gives 0,1,2,3. Each value is squared: 0²=0, 1²=1, 2²=4, 3²=9. Output: 0, 1, 4, 9 on separate lines.

questions.question:
What is the output?
nums = [2, 3, 5]
total = 0
for num in nums:
    total = total + num
print(total)
questions.explanation: Loops through the list accumulating: 0+2=2, 2+3=5, 5+5=10. Prints 10. This is the standard pattern for summing a list with a for loop.
for key in dict iterates over keys. When key is "Bob", student_marks["Bob"] retrieves the value 75. This is the pattern for looping through a dictionary and accessing a specific key's value.
range(start, stop, step) with step=2 skips every other number. Starts at 1, next is 3, next would be 5 but 5 is not < 5. So only 1 and 3 are printed.
Loops over all keys, accessing and summing values: 10+20+30=60. total += data[key] is shorthand for total = total + data[key].
range(3, 0, -1) counts DOWN from 3, stopping before reaching 0. Prints 3, 2, 1. A negative step makes range count backwards.

questions.question:
What is the output?
nums = [1, 2, 3]
for i in range(len(nums)):
    nums[i] = nums[i] * 2
print(nums)
questions.explanation: range(len(nums)) gives indices 0,1,2. Each element is doubled in place: nums[0]=2, nums[1]=4, nums[2]=6. The original list is mutated.
range(0, 10, 3) gives: 0, 3, 6, 9 — stops before reaching 10. That is 4 values, so the body runs 4 times.
Loops over both keys. For Alice: sum([85,90])=175. For Bob: sum([70,75])=145. print(key, total) outputs the key and its sum on the same line.

questions.question: What keyword defines a function in Python?
questions.explanation: Python uses def to define a function. Syntax: def function_name(parameters): followed by an indented body. Unlike JavaScript there is no function keyword or curly braces — indentation defines the body.
2000 % 400 == 0 is True, making the whole condition True → returns True. Year 2000 is a leap year because it is divisible by 400.
1900 % 4 == 0 is True, BUT 1900 % 100 == 0 is also True, so not(year%100==0) is False — first part fails. 1900 % 400 != 0 — second part fails. Returns False. 1900 is NOT a leap year.

questions.question:
What is the output?
number = 3.14159
print("%.2f" % number)
questions.explanation: %.2f formats a float to exactly 2 decimal places. 3.14159 rounded to 2 decimals: the third decimal is 1 (below 5), so it rounds down → 3.14.

questions.question:
What is the output?
number = 7.0
print(f"{number:.2f}")
questions.explanation: :.2f in an f-string always shows exactly 2 decimal places, even for whole numbers. 7.0 becomes 7.00. This is equivalent to "%.2f" % number and "{:.2f}".format(number).
end="" replaces the default newline with nothing. All values print on the same line with no separator: 123.
end="-" appends a dash AFTER each number, including the last. Output is 1-2-3- with a trailing dash. If you want 1-2-3 without the trailing dash, you'd use "-".join() instead.

questions.question: What does a Python function return if it has no return statement?
questions.explanation: Python functions without a return statement automatically return None. This is Python's equivalent of JavaScript's undefined for functions.

questions.question:
What is the output?
number = 2.675
print("%.2f" % number)
questions.explanation: Due to floating-point representation, 2.675 is stored as slightly less than 2.675 in memory. So it rounds DOWN to 2.67, not up to 2.68. This is a classic floating-point precision trap.

questions.question:
What is the output?
def multiply(a, b):
    result = a * b

print(multiply(3, 4))
questions.explanation: The function computes a * b but never returns it. Without a return statement, the function returns None. print(None) outputs None.

questions.question: What does `"-".join(["a", "b", "c"])` return?
questions.explanation: .join() concatenates list elements using the string as a separator. The separator appears BETWEEN elements only, not at the start or end. Result: a-b-c

questions.question:
What is the output?
L = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
L.append(9)
L.sort()
print(L)
questions.explanation: append(9) adds 9 to the end → [3,1,4,1,5,9]. sort() arranges in ascending order → [1,1,3,4,5,9].

questions.question:
What is the output?
L = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
L.pop()
L.reverse()
print(L)
questions.explanation: pop() removes the LAST element (5) → [1,2,3,4]. reverse() flips the list in place → [4,3,2,1].

questions.question: What is the key difference between a list and a tuple?

options.label: b
options.text: Lists are mutable (can change); tuples are immutable (fixed)

options.label: c
options.text: Lists are faster; tuples are slower

questions.explanation: Lists use [] and are mutable — elements can be added, removed, or changed. Tuples use () and are immutable — once created they cannot be modified. Tuples are also faster and use less memory.

questions.question: What does intersection (A ∩ B) return?

options.label: a
options.text: All elements from both sets combined

options.label: b
options.text: Elements only in A but not B

options.label: c
options.text: Elements that appear in BOTH A and B

options.label: d
options.text: Elements in A or B but not both

questions.explanation: Intersection returns only elements that exist in BOTH sets. If A={1,2,3} and B={2,3,4}, then A ∩ B = {2,3} — only what they share.
s1 ∩ s2 = {3, 4} — the elements present in both sets. Length = 2.
Union combines all unique elements. s1 ∪ s2 = {1,2,3,4,5} — element 3 appears in both but is counted once. Length = 5.

questions.question: What is the difference between `remove()` and `discard()` in sets?

options.label: b
options.text: remove() raises KeyError for missing elements; discard() does not

options.label: c
options.text: discard() raises KeyError; remove() does not

options.label: d
options.text: remove() adds an element; discard() deletes it

questions.explanation: Both remove an element. Key difference: remove() raises a KeyError if the element is missing. discard() silently does nothing if the element is not found. Use discard() when unsure if the element exists.

difference returns elements in s1 that are NOT in s2. s1 has {1,2,3,4}, remove {3,4} → {1,2}. Length = 2.
Symmetric difference = elements in either set but NOT in both. s1 has {1,2} not in s2. s2 has {5,6} not in s1. Together = {1,2,5,6}. Length = 4.

questions.question: What does `s.pop()` do on a set?

options.label: c
options.text: Removes and returns an arbitrary element

s2 - s1 = {4,5} (in s2 but not in s1) → len 2. s1 - s2 = {} (every element of s1 is in s2) → len 0. Difference is NOT symmetric — order matters!

questions.question: What does `Counter` from collections do?

options.label: a
options.text: Counts the total number of elements

options.label: c
options.text: Counts how many times each element appears

questions.explanation: Counter creates a dictionary-like object where keys are elements and values are their counts. Counter([1,2,2,3,3,3]) gives {3:3, 2:2, 1:1}.

questions.question: What are exceptions in Python?

options.label: a
options.text: Errors caught before the program runs

options.label: d
options.text: Warnings that don't stop the program

questions.explanation: Exceptions are errors detected during execution — they happen while your program is running, not before it starts.

questions.question: Which error is raised when the second argument of a division operation is zero?
questions.explanation: ZeroDivisionError is raised when the second argument of a division or modulo operation is zero — for example, 1 / 0.

questions.question: A ValueError is raised when a built-in function receives an argument that has the right type but...

options.label: d
options.text: is a float instead of int

questions.explanation: A ValueError is raised when a built-in operation receives an argument with the right type but an inappropriate value — for example, int('#') receives a string (correct type) but '#' cannot be converted to an integer.

questions.question: What keyword is used to begin a block of code that might raise an exception?
questions.explanation: The 'try' keyword starts the block of code you want to monitor for exceptions. If an error occurs inside it, Python looks for a matching except clause.

questions.question: What keyword is used to catch an exception?
questions.explanation: The 'except' keyword catches exceptions. It follows the try block and specifies which exception type to handle.

questions.question: What does int('0') return?
questions.explanation: '0' is a valid digit string, so int('0') successfully converts it to the integer 0. No exception is raised.

questions.question: What does int('#') raise?
questions.explanation: int('#') raises a ValueError because '#' has the right type (string) but is not a valid integer value — it cannot be converted to a number.

questions.question: What error does 1 / 0 raise in Python?
questions.explanation: Dividing by zero raises ZeroDivisionError. The error message is 'integer division or modulo by zero'.

questions.question: Which of the following would raise a ZeroDivisionError?
questions.explanation: ZeroDivisionError is raised when the second argument of a division OR modulo operation is zero. 10 % 0 uses zero as the divisor in modulo, so it raises ZeroDivisionError. 0 / 10 is fine because 10 is not zero.

questions.question: What does 'as e' do in:  except ZeroDivisionError as e:

options.label: c
options.text: Assigns the exception object to variable e

options.label: d
options.text: Raises a new exception named e

questions.explanation: 'as e' binds the caught exception object to the variable e, so you can use e to access the error message — for example, printing 'Error Code:', e.

questions.question: Given a = '1' and b = '0', what does int(a) / int(b) raise?
questions.explanation: int('0') converts successfully to 0. Dividing int('1') / 0 then raises ZeroDivisionError, not ValueError, because the conversion itself succeeded.

questions.question: Given a = '1' and b = '#', what error is raised when evaluating int(a) / int(b)?
questions.explanation: int('#') fails first before any division happens, raising ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '#'. The division never gets a chance to run.

options.label: d
options.text: No output; the program crashes silently

questions.explanation: 1/0 raises ZeroDivisionError, which is caught by the except clause. The variable e holds the error message 'division by zero', so the output is: Error Code: division by zero.

questions.question: Can a try statement have more than one except clause?

options.label: a
options.text: No, only one except clause is allowed

options.label: b
options.text: Yes, to specify handlers for different exception types

options.label: d
options.text: Only when using a finally block

questions.explanation: A try statement may have more than one except clause to specify handlers for different exceptions — for example, one clause for ZeroDivisionError and another for ValueError.

questions.question: What does the zip() function return?
questions.explanation: zip() returns a list of tuples. The i-th tuple contains the i-th element from each of the argument sequences or iterables.

questions.question: What does the i-th tuple in zip()'s output contain?

options.label: a
options.text: The i-th element from only the first sequence

options.label: b
options.text: The i-th element from each argument sequence

options.label: c
options.text: All elements from the i-th sequence

options.label: d
options.text: The sum of all i-th elements

questions.explanation: The i-th tuple produced by zip() contains the i-th element from each of the argument sequences or iterables passed to it.

questions.question: What is the first tuple in the output of zip([1,2,3,4,5,6], 'Hacker')?
questions.explanation: zip() pairs elements by position. The 0th element of [1,2,3,4,5,6] is 1, and the 0th character of 'Hacker' is 'H', so the first tuple is (1, 'H').

questions.question: If two sequences have unequal lengths, what does zip() do?

options.label: b
options.text: Pads the shorter one with None

options.label: c
options.text: Truncates to the length of the shortest sequence

options.label: d
options.text: Extends to the length of the longest sequence

questions.explanation: If argument sequences are of unequal lengths, the returned list is truncated to the length of the shortest argument sequence.

questions.question: What is the full output of zip([1,2,3,4,5,6], 'Hacker')?
questions.explanation: zip() pairs each number with the character at the same position in 'Hacker'. Both have 6 elements, so all 6 pairs are produced: [(1,'H'),(2,'a'),(3,'c'),(4,'k'),(5,'e'),(6,'r')].

questions.question: What does zip(*X) do when X is a list of lists?

options.label: b
options.text: Unpacks X and zips the inner lists together

questions.explanation: The * operator unpacks X so each inner list becomes a separate argument to zip(). zip() then pairs elements from each inner list by position.

questions.question: What does any() return if at least one element of the iterable is True?
questions.explanation: any() returns True if any element of the iterable is true. Even if only one element is True, the result is True.

questions.question: What does any() return if the iterable is empty?
questions.explanation: The lesson states: If the iterable is empty, any() will return False. There are no elements to be true, so the result is False.

questions.explanation: all() returns True if all of the elements of the iterable are true. Every element must be true for all() to return True.
